summary.decision_made: Locked Phase 1 gameplay loop for the pure-chance drinking-game race. Single round = Lobby → Bidding → Setup → Race → Settlement → Distribution → Ready. 9 phases total. Bidding capped 1–5, all bids public, 30s distribution timer with uniform-random fallback, 4 horses, track 6–20.
options_considered: Pure-chance (chosen) vs. light influence lever for V1
Full hidden bids vs. public bids (chose public for social drama)
Bid cap 1–5 (chosen) vs. free-amount vs. multi-horse split
Random fallback policy: uniform random (chosen) vs. equal split vs. lowest-consumption target
summary.difficulties_encountered: Track-card flip rule (last-placed horse triggers) creates an asymmetric early/late-race dynamic; explicitly documented in Core Loop Step 5 and Glossary 'Step (last-placed horse triggers flip)' to avoid future confusion.
summary.key_takeaway: V1 is a pure-luck betting game where the only meaningful choice is bid-amount + post-race drinks distribution; deck-builder effect cards are explicitly deferred to V2.

summary.decision_made: Created a dedicated Future Concept Vault at .context/race_modifiers.md to capture expansion ideas (Energy Channeling, Track modifiers, Suit Synchronization) without scope-creeping the V1 prototype. AGENTS.md updated with a structured interview protocol for capturing and refining future modifier pitches.
options_considered: Dedicated vault file with structured schema (field descriptions, beneficiary, upgrades) — chosen
Keep ideas flattened in todo_random.md — rejected (too unstructured, no upgrade paths)
No formal capture, rely on memory — rejected (ideas get lost)
summary.difficulties_encountered: Balancing a schema structured enough for future design work but lightweight enough not to create friction during brainstorming. The current schema uses a 'fast-track' bypass clause for half-baked ideas.
summary.reversals_and_refactors: N/A — first implementation of this process.
summary.key_takeaway: Formalizing an ideation vault early prevents promising expansion concepts from either being forgotten or prematurely derailing the core prototype.

summary.decision_made: Selected Cloudflare Workers + Durable Objects + Pages for the V1 prototype. Per-room Durable Objects provide strong state consistency for the 9-phase state machine with concurrent player actions. WebSocket transport via DO's Hibernation API handles real-time sync for up to 20 players per room. No database needed — game state is ephemeral in DO memory. Room codes derived from DO IDs.
options_considered: Cloudflare Workers + Durable Objects + Pages (chosen) — true $0, per-room consistency, WebSocket native, edge-deployed, no DevOps
Bun/Hono on Fly.io — simpler single-process model, free tier but VMs sleep on inactivity causing ~5s cold starts
WebRTC P2P — pure peer-to-peer, no server cost, but NAT traversal and hosted-player model make it too complex for a prototype
SSE + POST — works on serverless but clunky bidirectional flow for bidding/distribution phases
summary.difficulties_encountered: Had to weigh per-room consistency guarantees against operational simplicity. The hosted player model (host manages bets/drinks for spectating players) eliminates WebRTC/P2P approaches because the server must be authoritative for both player types. Durable Objects' WebSocket Hibernation API solves the idle-room cost problem — inactive rooms cost nothing until a player reconnects.
summary.key_takeaway: Durable Objects' per-room isolation maps cleanly to the game's room-per-game model; the 9-phase state machine becomes a simple class inside a DO, with each WebSocket connection representing one player (or one host managing multiple hosted players).

summary.decision_made: Workers/DOs code lives in src/ (parallel to frontend/ for the Pages SPA). Both are siblings under the project root, reflecting their separate deployment targets — wrangler deploy for src/, wrangler pages deploy for frontend/.
options_considered: src/ (chosen) — standard Workers project convention, wrangler.toml defaults, and the `src/` vs `frontend/` pairing is immediately understood without explanation
backend/ — symmetric label with frontend/ but over-promises scope (no REST API, no DB, no auth middleware running here)
engine/ — matches the 'game engine' phrase but undersells the Worker HTTP/WS routing and DO orchestration layers
summary.difficulties_encountered: None — the decision was straightforward once the question was asked. The architecture doc had been vague about the exact layout.
summary.reversals_and_refactors: N/A — first time committing the repo layout to a decision document.
summary.key_takeaway: Naming the Workers directory src/ keeps the project approachable for anyone familiar with Cloudflare Workers conventions, and separates concerns cleanly from the frontend SPA.

summary.decision_made: Delivered full MVP vertical slice: pure-TS game engine (15 state-machine functions), Cloudflare Worker + Durable Object with WebSocket Hibernation API, and vanilla-TS Pages SPA. Race pacing uses self-rescheduling DO alarms (750ms/draw) rather than a synchronous loop — avoids blocking the handler and aligns with the platform's timer idiom used for bid/dist/ready deadlines.
options_considered: Synchronous race loop in webSocketMessage handler — rejected; would hold the inbound-message invocation open for 60+s and risk duration guards
Self-rescheduling alarms (chosen) — one draw per alarm tick, returns from handler immediately, matches existing bid/dist/ready timer pattern
Room code passed via X-Room-Code header from Worker to DO — DO derives identity from idFromName but needs the human-readable code for state_sync payloads
host_set_track_length WS message added to B1 contract — plan omission caught during implementation; 15-line fix across messages.ts + room.ts
summary.difficulties_encountered: Track-card flip / regression rule (Step 5) is the most subtle engine path: 'regression is not a re-passing' means one draw → at most one flip + one regression, no cascading. WebSocket Hibernation API requires compat flag removal (web_socket_hibernation doesn't exist as a flag; hibernation is native in modern Workers). Browser tool sandbox can't reach localhost for D5 smoke — relied on C4 eval smoke (create room → WS → bid → race streaming → settlement) as verification gate.
summary.reversals_and_refactors: Advisory-driven fix: race pacing redesigned from synchronous loop to alarm-driven before room.ts was written. Room code forwarding added mid-implementation (DO didn't receive the code). collision detection in /init fixed from always-200 to 409-on-collision.
summary.key_takeaway: A Durable Object game server is a state machine redux in the constructor + alarm() dispatch — the 9-phase loop reads as a switch on room.state with timer-driven transitions. The RNG abstraction (interface + SeededRNG for tests) kept the engine deterministic and fully testable without a Workers runtime.

milestone: UI Overhaul + Race Pacing + Host Controls

summary.decision_made: Delivered 14 changes from V1 post-playtest UX audit: (1) two-stage race alarm (DRAW/FLIP) with per-room configurable pacing (raceGapDeckMs/raceGapTrackMs, default 2000/1000), replacing hardcoded 750ms constant — gap lands between deck-draw and track-flip so spectators see a breath between moves; (2) SETTLEMENT phase now holds at the race-end snapshot instead of auto-advancing to DISTRIBUTION — host clicks Continue to advance; (3) home screen split into sequential name-then-room flow with sessionStorage persistence; (4) lobby restructured with player list first, inline host bid controls for hosted players, pacing editor, rename-in-place for self and hosted players, Remove button replacing Kick; (5) RacingView replaced absolute-positioned dots with a CSS-grid text track using ♘ knights in per-suit colours (gold Coins, brown Cups, gray Swords, green Clubs); (6) race log grouped into turn-delimited blocks by DECK_DRAW events; (7) new ResultsView shows final track, placements, settlement, player list; (8) DoneView now persists the player list; (9) host_set_bid message added for frontend clarity; (10) change_name and host_set_player_name messages for lobby renames.
options_considered: Two-stage alarm with private pendingStage field (chosen) — avoids polluting the shared Room type, survives DO eviction between races gracefully, no schema migration needed
Single-stage alarm with an in-room _pendingDraw field — rejected because structuredClone in the game machine would drop it on every edit; plan explicitly called for private DO field
Continuous synchronous race loop — rejected in V1, remains rejected; DO duration guard kills 60s handlers
host_set_bid as a separate message from host_place_bid (chosen) — distinct naming for frontend clarity; identical server handler behind the scenes
Turn-delimited log via a new raceLogGrouped field on Room — rejected; computed at render time from the existing flat raceLog, zero wire cost
summary.difficulties_encountered: The ClientMessage union in shared/messages.ts lost host_set_track_length during the edit sequence (a SWAP misaligned with the actual line number because a prior import edit shifted line positions). Caught by tsc and fixed in the same pass. The test file's closing bracket structure similarly misaligned after inserting describe blocks — required a follow-up read-and-repair. The two-stage alarm is the most critical runtime path: if pendingStage desyncs from the actual room state (e.g. eviction mid-race), the next alarm safely restarts at DRAW because the default is null. The lobby rewrite was the largest single component (~300 lines replacing ~100) with nested per-row signals for bid selectors and rename inputs.
summary.reversals_and_refactors: The plan specified the SETTLEMENT phase not auto-advancing — in V1 it auto-advanced from RACING through SETTLEMENT straight to DISTRIBUTION. The alarm was the tightest integration point: the draw-step alarm sees SETTLEMENT from a 3rd-finish and now returns without scheduling further work. Race pacing removed the last hardcoded timing constant from the DO; every alarm schedule reads from room.raceGapDeckMs or raceGapTrackMs.
summary.key_takeaway: A DO game server whose core loop is 'draw then flip, each with its own gap' maps naturally to a two-state alarm machine — the drawer function and flipper function are pure, the alarm just picks which one to run and when. The frontend got heavier (SolidJS signals for per-row state in the lobby), but the wire protocol stayed lean: only three new client message types and zero new server message types.
